Sample topics include Gender and Diversity in Dance, Notable Choreographers and Dancers, Dance in Pop Culture, Cultural Dance as a form of Expression for Indigenous Peoples, Global Exploration in Dance, Dance in Film and Literature, and Female Pioneers of Ballet and Modern Dance.

title: Internship
description: Contact department for more information.

title: Initial Practice Seminar
description: Reflective practice seminar to accompany initial field experiences in elementary, special education, middle school and secondary education settings. Students will observe and discuss instructional practices and classroom management strategies that contribute to the development of all learners, share and discuss student case studies, develop an initial philosophy of teaching, and construct a professional development plan.

title: Econometrics
description: Statistical methods used in economics and other behavioral sciences emphasizing simple and multiple linear regression analysis.

title: Survey of Economics
description: This course introduces the essentials of economics, divided between microeconomics and macroeconomics. Students will examine how individuals and firms make decisions under various market conditions, then explore broader economic phenomena such as inflation, unemployment, and the effects of fiscal and monetary policy. This course blends theory with real-world applications, preparing students across all disciplines to understand and navigate the economic forces at play in everyday life and policy making.

title: Integrated Science for Teachers
description: This course will provide teacher candidates with an opportunity to explore the science content that is the foundation of the K-8 Ohio Science academic content standards, including astronomy, biology, physics, engineering, and chemistry.

title: Intermediate Microeconomics
description: This course focuses on the following topics: basic theory of consumer behavior; production and costs; partial equilibrium analysis of pricing in competitive and monopolistic markets; game theory; and social welfare analysis.

title: Integrated Social Studies for Teachers
description: This course will provide teacher candidates with an opportunity to explore the social studies content that is the foundation of the K-8 Ohio Social Studies academic content standards, including history, geography, economics, and political science.

title: Writing for Teachers
description: An advanced writing education course designed to assist students begin to switch their focus from self-as-student to self-as-writing teacher. We will explore and learn that to teach writing, we must write. We will do this through reading; discussing accounts by professional writers, student writers, teachers of writing and writing researchers; exploring the teaching of writing in the classroom; creating a community of writers where we write and respond to each others' writing; understanding the importance of reading and writing and that to teach both we must be active readers and writers; experiencing an environment created around the social construction of knowledge; and integrating content knowledge from the various liberal arts disciplines that are the foundation of our knowledge.

title: Environmental & Natural Resource Economics
description: The economic analysis of exhaustible and renewable resources. Topics include the design and impact of government policy as it pertains to water and air pollution, global warming, endangered species, sustainable economic growth, and a host of other environmental problems.

title: Intermediate Macroeconomics
description: Integrated study of Neoclassical economics and Keynesian theory of income and employment. Applications and limitations of Keynesian economics. Monetarist theory and post-Keynesian developments.

title: Initial Practice Clinical Experience
description: Initial clinical experiences in elementary, special education, middle school and secondary education settings. Students will observe instructional practices and classroom management strategies that contribute to the development of all learners. They will use their observations and experience to share and discuss student case studies, develop an initial philosophy of teaching, and construct a professional development plan in the associated seminar course.

title: Integrated Arts
description: Drawing upon a theoretical perspective, this course emphasizes methods and materials for integrating movement, visual arts, and music across the curriculum in the elementary classroom. The course will focus on developmentally appropriate materials, methods, and effective practices for working with all learners. Investigation and practice in the incorporation of the arts across the curriculum.

title: Foundations of Reading
description: This course provides a scientifically based foundation in the cognitive, socio-cultural, linguistic, and motivational influences on literacy and language development. The course presents the key scientifically based reading research foundations needed to understand how reading develops and effective methods and strategies used to teach literacy skills to young children through young adults. Topics include understanding reading research, cognitive psychology’s contributions to understanding the reading process, language development, the sequence of learning to read, the essential components of reading instruction, and an introduction to the most effective approaches to teaching reading across the grade bands.

title: Technology in the Classroom
description: Emphasizes the use of computers and new media resources in pre-K-12. Students gain a working knowledge of the educational technology tools most frequently used in PK-12 classrooms by teachers, as well as an understanding of how technology may be used to support diverse learners and English language learners. Digital citizenship and online safety, for both teachers and PK-12 students, is also covered.

title: Educational Psychology
description: Human development and behavior of individual (both typical and non-typically developing) from birth through adolescence as affected by heredity, development, and training. Emphasizes research- based and practical aspects of readiness, aptitude interests, and social adjustments, as well as findings of modern theories of learning related to children. Ten hours of field and/or clinical experience. Additional fees apply.

title: Early Childhood Transition Seminar
description: This seminar course focuses on strategies and sample methodologies in transitioning special education services for Birth through Grade 5. Effective collaboration with other professionals, parents and caregivers in the planning and delivery of services will be emphasized in a classroom field setting along with observation and interactions with typically and non-typically developing children. Includes 20 field experience hours in an inclusive preschool setting.

title: Secondary Transition Planning
description: The course will provide teacher education candidates (MCSED, AYA dual, and WESED majors) a variety of strategies and sample methodologies in functional academics and social/life, and employment skills for high school students with disabilities who are of transition age (secondary learners with intellectual and/or physical, sensory disabilities). A comprehensive portfolio will be required for this course. This course requires 20 clinical contact hours in The Pioneer Pipeline on campus.

title: Principles of Math
description: Principles of Mathematics is designed to provide teacher candidates with a deeper understanding of the mathematics they will teach in grades Pre-Kindergarten through eight as aligned with Common Core State Standards and Ohio Learning Standards for Mathematics. Common areas for remediation of incomplete learning, as well as methods to support progress toward mastery of these concepts, are also covered. The course emphasizes conceptual development and a deep understanding of the foundations of mathematical principles, logic, and reasoning to support teacher candidates with the ability to teach content conceptually. There is a focus on error analysis as a means to understand non-standard methods grade PK - 8 students may use and to evaluate those methods for conceptual accuracy.

title: Children's Literature
description: Introduction to literature for early and middle childhood, including picture books, traditional literature, poetry, fiction, and nonfiction. Emphasis in integration of literature across the early and middle childhood curriculum, the use of technology as a tool for enhancing literature study, and the use of multicultural works in the classroom.

title: Classroom Management
description: Investigation of various approaches to classroom  organization, strategies to promote appropriate  behavior and to prevent, modify, and extinguish  inappropriate and disruptive behavior. Strategies  for accommodating diversity among students and working with families. Field experience required in conjunction with class.

title: Ohio History for Teachers
description: General look at Ohio history, geography, environmental factors, governance, economics, global connections and the effects of technology on society. Focus on teaching methods that are appropriate for those teaching Ohio History at the 4th to 7th grade levels.

title: History of American Education
description: Overview of each major period in American education against the backdrop of world and national events from 1600 through present day. Analysis of how historical developments have shaped the twenty-first century American public and private school systems.

title: Reading Field Experience: Early Childhood
description: Planned program of work in a reading classroom under the direction of a certified/licensed reading teacher to gain experience with instructional strategies in teaching reading. Stu- dents will observe and teach in a reading class- room, create authentic tasks for reading instruction, develop communication skills, gain experience with word recognition and comprehension strategies, participate in student assessments, and utilize available technology. (Graded "satisfactory/unsatisfactory.") Open only to students under the 2016-2017 or previous curriculum.

title: Diverse Learners
description: A study of exceptional learners across their life span, services, legal considerations, research, current issues and trends, and parent/family roles rights, and responsibilities. Emphasizes collaboration and methods of instruction among inclusionary teachers, specialists, related professionals, parents/families, and community agencies. Planned program of work on classrooms and/or community agencies serving exceptional learners from a wide ability range: at-risk, mild to moderate, moderate to severe, and gifted. Additional fees apply.

title: Foundations of Wellness Education
description: This course introduces the historical, philosophical, and sociological impact of educational policy for health and physical education. A perspective of wellness education emerges as an inclusive and interdisciplinary model for meeting the holistic needs of all learners. This course introduces a paradigm shift in education and the need for educators to meet each child’s cognitive, physical, social, and emotional development through wellness education.

title: Instructional Design
description: Introduction to lesson and unit planning with emphasis on writing unit goals and lesson objectives aligned with academic content standards and assessments. Additional focus on differentiation of lesson activities and assessments, interdisciplinary planning, incorporation of performance arts, and use of evidence-based instructional practices for students in Pre-K through grade 5.

title: Reading Field Experience: Intervention Specialist
description: Planned program of work in an elementary or middle school inclusion classroom or resource room under the direction of a certified/licensed teacher to gain experience with instructional strategies in teaching reading to students with mild to moderate exceptionalities. Students will create authentic tasks for reading instruction, develop communications skills, gain experience with word recognition and comprehension strategies, participate in student assessments and utilize available technology. (Graded "satisfactory/unsatisfactory.")

title: Standards for Wellness Education
description: This course examines the intersection of state and national standards for physical education and social-emotional learning from preschool through 12th grade. Teacher candidates apply their knowledge by designing developmentally appropriate activities aligned to standards, interdisciplinary content, and wellness education for the whole child.

title: Assessment
description: Information and skills teachers need on a variety of evaluation measures used to assess children.  Students develop skills necessary to align standards, objectives, learning tasks, and assessment.  Participants will be asked to collect baseline data and then apply quantitative measures in order to make decisions and engage in data-driven decision making.  Ten hours of field experience is required for this course.

title: Assessment of Mild/Moderate Learners
description: Formal and non-formal evaluation methods to assess students with mild/moderate disabilities, which include standardized and state and federal mandated alternative assessment practices. The role of the assessment in the IEP and multi-factored evaluation process.

title: Trauma Informed Schools & Classrooms
description: This course will cover traumatic issues regarding children and the impact it has on adulthood. Special attention will be paid to giving educators and community leaders strategies to assist children to learn in the classroom and how trauma impacts families and community longitudinally over the lifespan. Types of trauma discussed are marital discord, community violence, domestic violence, sex trafficking, serious medical illness, neglect, displacement and refugee trauma, school violence, sexual abuse, terrorism, loss of a loved one/grief, psychological maltreatment, and family dysfunction. Additional fees apply.

title: Principles of Phonics
description: Overview of the phonological and morphological elements which comprise standard written English. Instructional strategies which facilitate the use of graphophonic cueing, word analysis, and sight word recognition for beginning though mature readers. Emphasis on the intensive, systematic integration of phonics assessment and instruction within the context of meaningful literacy experiences among typically and non-typically developing learners.

title: General Methods
description: Theoretical, research-based, and practical aspects of classroom teaching. Classroom management, planning, evaluation, and teaching strategies which emphasize technology integration, team planning, investigative teaching methods for early adolescents through adults, and collaboration with parents, other professionals, and community agencies. This is a clinical course requiring 30 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Teaching Reading in the Content Area
description: Instructional principles and practices for helping middle and high school students and adults learn subject matter through the application of reading, writing, and study skills.

title: Assistive Technology for Students with Disabilities
description: This course reviews the technology used to assist individuals with disabilities in accessing general education curriculum and activities, as well as technology to assist people with disabilities in everyday activities. Students are provided with the knowledge and skills needed to use technology to support individuals in computer access, augmentative and alternative communication systems, reading, writing, and everyday activities. Assessment and implementation issues are presented in the context of 15 hours in the field with students. Requires 15 hours in the field in addition to weekly 50-minute seminar.

title: Instructional Methods Mild/Moderate Learners
description: Methods for designing and implementing instruction for students with mild/moderate disabilities. Topics include individualized programming, designing and managing environments and materials, critical teaching behaviors, introduction to data-based assessment and skills and knowledge strategy in mathematics, reading, and writing. Students will participate in a variety of activities to demonstrate the skills and knowledge acquired in the classroom setting. Additional fees apply.

title: Wellness Education Methods (PreK-5)
description: Methods for designing and implementing instruction for wellness education for PreK-5th grade levels. Teacher candidates will develop best practices in planning, instruction, assessment and reflection. This clinical methods course requires 40 hours of field experience under the supervision of the course instructor and licensed mentor teacher.

title: Wellness Ed Methods (6th-12th)
description: Methods for designing and implementing instruction for wellness education for 6th -12th grade levels. Teacher candidates will develop best practices in planning, instruction, assessment and reflection. This clinical methods course requires 40 hours of field experience under the supervision of the course instructor and licensed mentor teacher.

title: Classroom Management & Behavior Supports
description: Focuses on positive behavioral supports, practices and strategies for children, youth and young adults with mild/moderate disabilities. Included in this course are effective practices for monitoring, assessing, evaluating, managing, structuring and promoting appropriate social interactions of learners with high incidence disabilities.

title: Classroom Management & Behavioral Assessment
description: This course focuses on positive behavioral supports, practices and strategies for children, youth, and young adults. Included in this course are effective practices for monitoring, assessing, evaluating, managing, structuring, and promoting appropriate social interactions of learners. Additionally, multiple academic and behavioral assessments will be explored.

title: Diagnosis & Correction of Reading Difficulties
description: Diagnosis of reading abilities and problems that may be exhibited by beginning through mature readers. Clinical experience with formal and informal reading assessment tools. Developmentally appropriate corrective techniques with special emphasis on literature-based and integrated language arts instruction. Additional fees apply.

title: Professional Practices Seminar
description: Investigation and application of the professional code of ethics for educators, skills for creating a professional resume, effective interviewing techniques, and professional license application procedures. Study of state compliance modules for educators including teacher and district use of value-added data, Ohio teacher evaluation system, school operating standards, standards for professional development, and resident educator program protocol and expectations. Completion of a professional development plan.

title: Education Law & Policy
description: Legal and political issues governing K-12 education in America. The role of federal and state statutes and case law in the structure and operation of schools. Analysis of the philosophical basis of traditional public schools and alternative models. Identification of educational and societal issues affecting schools, teachers, and learners and analysis of potential solutions.

title: Developmental Literacy for Diverse Students
description: A comprehensive exploration of the developmental and instructional implications related to fluent reading, vocabulary acquisition, comprehension and writing instruction. Along with these core aspects of reading, major skill domains that contribute to the development of written expression are explored. Principles of explicit and systematic instruction of these core aspects of reading and writing are studied and practiced.

title: Content Area Reading in the Middle Grades
description: Instructional principles and practices for helping  middle grade students learn subject matter  through the application of reading, writing and  study skills. Emphasis on addressing general and  content-specific literacy strategies and materials

title: Early Childhood Education Science Methods
description: Content, materials, methods, assessment techniques, technology, and integrative approaches to instruction in PK-3. The processes of scientific investigation are studies through inquiry, cooperative learning, multi-sensory activities, and various manners of collecting and analyzing data. Conceptual connections across disciplines are stressed for real-world situations.  Differing learning modalities and multiculturalism in the sciences are addressed.  This is a clinical course requiring 20 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Middle School Concept
description: Historical development, philosophy and the competencies needed in the middle school. Course includes research and study of exemplary middle school curricula, instructional and organizational practices that are developmentally appropriate for early adolescent learners, communication strategies effective for large and small groups of middle level students, interdisciplinary teaming, assessment strategies appropriate for early adolescent learners, and working collaboratively with families, professionals, and community groups. Additional fees apply.

title: Culture & Language in the Classroom
description: This course is taken concurrently with the full time internship. The course investigates multicultural and language issues as they impact the teaching/learning environment. It broadens the intern's understanding of diversity by identifying, adapting to, and embracing cultural and language differences among their students. Conducted primarily as a seminar throughout the semester, the course includes 40 hours of fieldwork in a multicultural setting in the student's licensing area and level.

title: Early Childhood Education Social Studies Methods
description: Purposes, organization, procedures, content, methods, and materials utilized in teaching social studies in grades P-3. Instruction will focus on examining national and state curricular standards for social studies through strategic instruction, such as: inquiry-based, project method, cooperative learning, concept mapping, integrative learning, and discussion techniques. Strategies and content will spotlight the need for students to be able to function in a continually changing world.  This is a clinical course requiring 20 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Inclusion/Special Education Seminar
description: Students in the pre-internship semester will take a deeper dive into IEP compliance and explore different strategies to engage their students in meaningful conversations, such as active listening, questioning techniques, and reflective dialogue. During class discussions, students will draw on their own experiences and thoughts to understand the special education process and how accommodations apply in the classroom. They will also share insights into the methods that they have observed in their clinical placements and discuss how these methods are being used to improve the educational outcomes of students with special needs. This course will have assigned experiences tied to the pre-internship clinical placement.

title: Early Childhood Education Mathematics Methods
description: Content, materials, methods, and assessment techniques used in early education to help a wide variety of learners (at-risk, mild to moderate, moderate to sever, gifted, culturally diverse, etc.) to discover and understand basic mathematical concepts through an integrated approach to instruction.  Focus on examining national and state curriculum standards; investigating a wide variety of methods and collaborative models used in early childhood mathematics; integrating technology; planning and implementing developmentally appropriate instruction using investigative, problem-solving, inquiry, and hands-on activities; and applying evaluative techniques for assessment of instruction.  This is a clinical course requiring 20 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Professional Education Capstone Seminar
description: Capstone seminar in education covering theoretical and pragmatic issues encountered in the P-12 classroom. Topics include accommodating diverse learners, assessment, classroom management, planning, teacher professionalism, career entry and creation and presentation of a capstone portfolio to demonstrate professional knowledge, skills, and dispositions.

title: Elementary Social Studies & Writing
description: Clinical course focusing on social studies curriculum, instructional methods, and materials for teaching students in kindergarten through fifth grade. Integration of writing instruction with social studies content and content rich reading materials.  Emphasis on strategies and materials that address the learning of all children. Includes 50 hours of pre-internship clinical experience in a K-5 inclusion classroom under the mentorship of a licensed teacher.

title: Math Methods
description: Mathematical teaching strategies, materials selection, technology integration, task development, student organization, and methods of assessment to create an environment conducive for teaching mathematical concepts through problem solving, investigation, and hands-on learning with manipulatives. Focus on teaching and assessing students of varying cultural backgrounds and learning styles through such things as cooperative learning, peer teaching, oral and written discourse, and portfolio development with respect to national, state and local curricular standards. This is a clinical course requiring 30 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Language Arts Methods
description: Curriculum construction and special methods in the teaching of language arts with respect to national state, and local student outcomes. Methodology and strategies for selecting, integrating, and translating knowledge of language arts disciplines for instruction in relation to national, state and local curriculum standards. Emphasizes communication, critical thinking strategies, assessment strategies, and the interrelationship of disciplines. This is a clinical course requiring 30 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Educational Studies Capstone
description: Culminating experience for the educational studies major. Required 60-hour practicum working with an education-related entity, non-profit agency, or museum. Weekly written and oral discussion of observations related to the practicum setting. Development and presentation of a proposal to address an identified need, issue, or area for enhancement at the practicum setting. Additional fees apply.

title: Instructional Methods for Elem STEM
description: Clinical course focusing on math and science curriculum, instructional methods, and materials for teaching students in kindergarten through fifth grade. Emphasis on the integration of developmentally appropriate science, technology, engineering, and mathematics concepts. Inclusion of strategies and materials that address culturally and academically diverse learners. Includes 50 hours of pre-internship clinical experience in a K-5 inclusion classroom under the mentorship of a licensed teacher.

title: Middle Childhood Social Studies Methods
description: Curriculum construction, design and study of  methods for teaching middle grades social studies  in a variety of locations and cultural settings.  Methodology and strategies for selecting,  integrating and translating knowledge of social  science disciplines for instruction in a relation  to national, state and local curriculum standards.  Emphasizes critical thinking strategies, formal  and informal assessment of strategies, the  interrelationship of disciplines and the multi- cultural aspects of social studies teaching. This is a clinical course requiring 30 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Middle Childhood Mathematics Methods
description: Mathematical teaching strategies, materials  selection, technology integration, task  development, student organization and methods of  assessment to create an environment conducive  for teaching mathematical concepts through  problem solving, investigation and hands-on  learning with manipulatives. Focus on teaching and assessing students of varying cultural backgrounds and learning styles through such things as  cooperative learning, peer teaching, oral and  written discourse and portfolio development with  respect to national, state and local middle grade mathematics standards. This is a clinical course requiring 30 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Secondary English/Language Arts Instructional Methods
description: Curriculum construction and special methods in the teaching of middle childhood and AYA language arts with respect to national, state and local student outcomes. Methodology and strategies for selecting, integrating and translating knowledge of language arts disciplines for instruction in relation to national, state and local curriculum standards. Course includes clinical components in the appropriate grade band as well as through online simulated modules (SimSchool).

title: Social Studies Methods
description: Curriculum construction, design, and study of special methods for teaching social studies in a variety of locations and cultural settings. Methodology and strategies for selecting, integrating, and translating knowledge of social science disciplines for instruction in relation to national, state, and local curriculum standards. Emphasizes critical thinking strategies, formal an informal assessment strategies, the interrelationship of disciplines and the multi- cultural aspects of social studies teaching. This is a clinical course requiring 30 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Science Methods
description: Curriculum construction and special methods of teaching science with regard to national, state, and local curriculum standards. Focus on inquiry- based teaching methods, laboratory planning, safety, and environmental concerns, the technology appropriate for science classrooms, and use and care of life specimens and subjects. Professional organizations, career opportunities, discipline ethics, and continuing professional development are included. This is a clinical course requiring 30 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Secondary Social Studies Instructional Methods
description: This course focuses on secondary social studies curriculum, instructional methods, and materials. The various course frameworks of social studies (history, civics, geography, government) will be examined. Active pedagogical frameworks for social studies instruction (TCI framework, C3 Framework, inquiry, etc.) will be examined. Course includes clinical components in the appropriate grade band as well as through online simulated modules (SimSchool).

title: Student Teaching (Early Childhood)
description: Fourteen week supervised professional teaching experience in the greater Washington County, Ohio area and under the guidance of a college supervisor and a licensed cooperating teacher(s) in early childhood education. Students are required to follow school district calendar. Additional fees apply.

title: Middle Childhood Science Methods
description: Curriculum construction and special methods of  teaching middle grades science with regard to  national, state and local curriculum standards.  Focus on inquiry-based teaching methods,  laboratory planning, safety and environmental  concerns, the technology appropriate for science  classrooms and use and care of life specimens and  subjects. Professional organizations, career  opportunities, discipline ethics and continuing  professional development are included. This is a clinical course requiring 30 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Student Teaching (Intervention Specialist)
description: Fourteen week supervised professional teaching experience in the greater Washington County, Ohio area and under the guidance of a college professor, an a licensed cooperating teacher(s) in an intervention setting with students with mild to moderate exceptionalities. Students are required to follow school district calendar. Additional fees apply.

title: Clinical Teaching Practicum
description: Twelve-week supervised teaching in classroom setting in the licensure band/content area under the guidance of a licensed teacher or administrator and college supervisor. Online observations and meetings with college supervisor for the purpose of feedback and assessment. Seminar discussion with teacher candidates and course instructor scheduled weekly throughout the experience.

title: Middle Childhood Language Arts Methods
description: Curriculum construction and special methods in  the teaching of middle childhood language arts  with respect to national, state and local student  outcomes. Methodology and strategies for  selecting, integrating and translating knowledge  of language arts disciplines for instruction in  relation to national, state and local curriculum  standards. Emphasizes communication, critical  thinking strategies, assessment strategies and  the interrelationship of disciplines. This is a clinical course requiring 30 hours of field experience in a content classroom under the supervision of the course instructor and a licensed classroom teacher.

title: Student Teaching (Middle Childhood)
description: Fourteen week supervised professional teaching experience in the greater Washington County, OH area and under the guidance of a college professor, and a licensed cooperating teacher(s) in middle childhood education. Students are required to follow the school district calendar. Additional fees apply.

title: Wellness/Special Education Internship
description: Fourteen week supervised professional teaching experience in the greater Washington County, Ohio area and under the guidance of a college supervisor and licensed intervention and wellness education mentor teachers in an inclusion classroom. Students are required to follow school district calendar. The internship experience will be shared within (50%) Intervention Specialist and (50%) Wellness Education (Physical Education) contexts.

title: Project Management
description: Introduction to the theory and practice of project management: conception and initiation; scope, definition and planning; performance and control; completion and evaluation.

title: Fluid Mechanics
description: Mechanical properties of fluids; fluid statics, mass balance, fluid and pressure measurement devices, and energy balance; Bernoulli's equation; laminar and turbulent flow; friction; machinery (pumps, compressors, and turbines). Laboratory work is included.

title: Student Teaching (Adolescent/Young Adult)
description: Fourteen week supervised professional teaching experience in the greater Washington County, OH area in a supervised placement in the student's area and at the appropriate grade level. Supervision shall be done by a Marietta College faculty member and a licensed cooperating teacher(s). Students are required to follow the school district calendar. Additional fees apply.

title: Economic Analysis & Investment Decision Methods
description: Determination of economic parameters such as rate of return, discounted present worth, future worth, annual worth, incremental rate of return and benefit/cost ratio; application of time value of money considerations to before and after-tax, evaluation of virtually all types of investment situations, emphasizing factors that influence management decisions; risk and uncertainty; depreciation; income tax calculations.

title: Thermodynamics & Transport
description: Energy and its transformation. Energy analysis of engineering systems, including power and refrigeration cycles. Includes first and second laws; equations of state; available and unavailable energy; transport phenomena.

title: Middle Childhood Dual Preparation Internship
description: Fourteen week supervised professional teaching experience in the greater Washington County, Ohio area and under the guidance of a college supervisor and licensed intervention and general education mentor teachers in an inclusion classroom. Students are required to follow school district calendar.

title: Elementary Dual Preparation Internship
description: Fourteen week supervised professional teaching experience in the greater Washington County, Ohio area and under the guidance of a college supervisor and licensed intervention and general education mentor teachers in an inclusion classroom. Students are required to follow school district calendar. Additional fees apply.

title: Literary Culture of the United States I
description: A survey of United States literature from the colonial period to the Civil War.

title: Engineering Statics
description: An introduction to the static analysis of mechanical systems. Topics include forces, vectors, torque, equilibrium analysis, structural analysis, friction, inertia, and centroids.

title: Strength of Materials
description: Principles of stress, strain, compressive loads, shear, torsion, transverse loads, eccentric loads, column mechanics, and combined stresses; emphasis on design. Materials covered include metals, wood, cements, and other porous media.

title: Secondary Science Instructional Methods
description: This course focuses on science curriculum, instructional methods, and materials for teaching students in grades four through twelve. There will be a focus on integration of writing instruction with science content. Emphasis on strategies and materials that address the learning of all children.

title: Literary Culture of Great Britain II
description: A survey of English literature of the 19th and 20th centuries.

title: Banned Books
description: What do books do? What (and who) are books for? These questions are critical for thinking about why books have been and continue to be banned, challenged, excised, and questioned. We will analyze a range of texts to explore the questions of what books do, what books are for, and who challenges and bans them—and why—and when.

title: Literary Culture of Great Britain I
description: A survey of English literature from the Middle Ages to the late 18th century.

title: Concepts of Gender
description: A study of cultural notions of gender and sexuality as they are reflected in and constructed by literary texts ranging from the classics to contemporary science fiction.

title: Literature Into Film
description: A comparative study of short stories, novels, and plays and their film adaptations.

title: Concepts of Nature
description: A historical study of the concept of nature as it is reflected in readings ranging from the Bible to contemporary poetry, drama and fiction.

title: Literary Culture of the United States II
description: A survey of the United States literature of the late 19th and 20th centuries.

title: Concepts of Postcolonialism
description: A study of cultural notions of power and identity  as they inform literature produced by specific  cultures emerging from colonialism in the  20th century.

title: Methods of Literary Study
description: This is the foundational course for English majors and minors. It includes an intense study of literary genres, techniques, terms, and themes, and emphasizes literary analysis, close reading, and research skills.  It is a prerequisite for ENGL 410 , the Senior Capstone.

title: Representative American Writers I
description: An examination of one American author or a combination of American authors who wrote during the 17th, 18th, or 19th centuries. May be repeated for credit under a different topic.

title: Concepts of Diversity
description: A study of concepts of diversity and "otherness"  as reflected in poetry, prose and film. Topics  may include race, ethnicity, socioeconomic  background, sexuality and religion.

title: Film as Literature
description: A comparative study of concepts of narration as they are reflected in literature and film.

title: Representative American Writers II
description: An examination of one American author or a combination of American authors who wrote during the 20th century. May be repeated for credit under a different topic.

title: Concepts of Comedy
description: A historical study of the concept of comedy as it is reflected in readings of poetry, drama, fiction, and criticism ranging from Plato to the present.

title: Representative British Writers I
description: An examination of one British author or a combination of British authors who wrote during the medieval period through the 18th century. May be repeated for credit under a different topic.

title: Studies in the Early British Novel
description: An examination of selected British novels by authors such as Fielding, Austen, and Hardy who shaped the development of the English-language novel through the late 19th century.

title: African American Literature
description: The class will study literature produced by African American authors and theoretical texts dealing with African American literature with a view toward developing a more complete understanding of American culture and literary movements, developing skills in analysis and interpretation, and enhancing writing skills.

title: Studies in the Early American Novel
description: An examination of selected American novels, by authors ranging from Brown to James, that represent the evolving tradition of novel writing in the United States from the late 18th century to 1900.

title: Topical Perspectives in Literature
description: An examination of the fiction, poetry, drama, and or film that represent a literary movement or critical approach to the study of literature. Topics, which will vary with each course offering, include the Harlem Renaissance, the pre-Raphaelites and historical images of women in literature.

title: Studies in British & American Poetry
description: An examination of significant poets and poetic traditions in British and American poetry.

title: Studies in the Later American Novel
description: An examination of selected American novels, by authors ranging from Faulkner to Morrison, that illustrate modernist and post-modernist technique.

title: Studies in the Later British Novel
description: An examination of selected British novels, by authors ranging from Lawrence to Byatt, that illustrate modernist and post-modernist technique.

title: Shakespeare
description: An examination, in the context of the entire canon of plays representative of the main periods of Shakespeare's development.

title: Senior Seminar
description: A seminar for senior English majors that focuses on the literature and literary background of a specific century. The topic focus will change each year. Together, students will examine a critical or historical text that offers a perspective on their literary research. Then each student will conduct research on one author of theme relevant to the century. This research should result in a significant paper, which will be presented in a public forum.

title: Representative British Writers II
description: An examination of one British author or a combination of British authors who wrote during the 19th and 20th centuries. May be repeated for credit under a different topic.

title: Internship
description: Registered via contract. See department for details. Graded Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory.

title: Energy Resources & Utilization Lab II
description: This laboratory course complements ENGY 103 by providing practice in the scientific method and hands-on experience in aspects of energy generation, transmission, conversion, and utilization. Familiarity with various energy conversion, monitoring, and auditing equipment will be gained. Formal reports, computer applications, and internet research are required.

title: Electrical Engineering
description: This course is an introduction to Electrical Engineering including circuits, electricity generation, and distribution and utilization from renewable as well as conventional methods. It focuses on the essential elements required for engineering students to pass the Fundamentals of Engineering exam. Knowledge of calculus (derivatives and integrals) is needed in order to gain the full benefit of this course. The course integrates lecture and laboratory experience. Additional fees apply.

title: Topical Perspectives in Film Study
description: An examination of a special topic related to film or the relationship of film and literature. Topics will vary with each course offering.

title: The Ethical Author: Literary Theory and Practice
description:
What role should literature play in human
communities? What responsibility do authors bear
toward their subjects, their readers, and their
art? When do aesthetic choices create ethical
dilemmas? Students will examine theoretical
questions about the nature and function of
literature in order to develop a deeper
understanding of the intersections of aesthetics
and ethics.

title: Energy Systems Design
description: This course will reinforce the social, economic and technical knowledge needed to analyze energy systems including both renewable and non-renewable sources. It will provide students with a practical experience designing an energy system that optimizes resources, generation, and use of energy. Students are given an opportunity to consider the utilization of a particular energy system and to analyze its environmental impact. The students will consider sustainability issues on both local and global scales and will compare and contrast the issues based on geo-social context. The course integrates lecture and applied experience.

title: Energy Resources & Utilization I
description: This course is designed to develop a student's understanding and awareness of conventionally used forms of energy, including fossil fuels and nuclear. In addition to these traditional fuel sources, renewable energy sources including wind, solar, hydrogen, geothermal and bio-energy will be covered. Economic environmental, social responsibility and ethical considerations will be incorporated in the course.

title: Energy Resources & Utilization Lab I
description: Basic experiments and demonstrations are assigned in order to illustrate principles covered in Energy Systems 101. Exercises are designed to advance the student's understanding of electricity, natural gas properties, gas storage applications and other forms of energy sources. Computer applications and internet research are learning tools used. Formal reports are required. Additional fees apply.

title: Creative Problem-Solving
description: This course is designed to cultivate and enhance creative thinking and problem-solving (CTPS) abilities. It addresses the need for individuals and teams who can 'think outside the box' and apply fresh perspectives to practical challenges. It delves into the essence of creativity and explores the traits of creative individuals, while identifying and addressing potential barriers to creativity. Students will be introduced to a variety of creative problem-solving techniques and methodologies for re-interpreting problems and generating ideas. This is an experiential course that includes practical demonstrations, exercises, and task simulations that enable students to gain a deeper understanding of these creative tools and the capacity to apply them and to facilitate their use to generate concepts and ideas.

title: Feasibility Analysis & Planning
description: Students enrolled in this course conduct a feasibility analysis and develop a plan to start a for-profit venture, a social enterprise, a non-profit organization, or a service club. Before taking this course, students must have identified the nature of the organization they would like to establish.

title: Energy Resources & Utilization II
description: This course builds on the knowledge of ENGY 101 by focusing on energy systems design which involves feasibility, economics and environmental impacts. Energy auditing techniques will be studied. Energy conservation and efficiency, developments in energy research, and employment opportunities will be explored.

title: Energy Conversion Principles
description: This course is designed for Energy Systems Engineering students. It will provide students with a basic understanding of energy conversion principles and technologies pertinent to both renewable and non-renewable sources.

title: Water and Wastewater Quality
description: Study of the preparation of water for domestic uses and treatment of wastewater for recycling or discharge into the environment. Topics include processes for removal of organics and inorganics, regulation of dissolved oxygen and techniques such as ion exchange, electrodialysis, reverse osmosis, activated carbon, adsorption and biological methods.

title: Air Quality
description: An overview of air quality, with a focus on emissions, chemistry, transport and other processes that govern dynamic behavior in the atmosphere. Quantitative assessment of the determining factors of air pollution, climate change, smog, measurement and air quality management techniques.

title: Entrepreneurial Thought & Action
description: This course equips students with the essential skills to think and behave like entrepreneurs. An entrepreneur possesses the ability to identify overlooked problems and convert them into opportunities for launching a new business or social enterprise or creating positive changes within an established organization by leveraging creative problem-solving techniques. Throughout this course, students will engage in experiential exercises and utilize tools such as the Root Cause Analysis and Business Model Canvas to cultivate new ideas and translate them into actionable initiatives to move forward under uncertain circumstances.

title: Introduction to Environmental Science
description: As a gateway to the Environmental Science and Environmental Studies degree programs, this course is intended for students who have two semesters of introductory level Biology, Geology, or Chemistry. The course covers sustainable development, demography and the impact of population on the environment, nonrenewable and renewable energy, toxicology, and risk analysis from social, economic, public policy and scientific perspectives.

title: Water & Wastewater Quality Laboratory
description: Laboratory course emphasizing techniques and basic principles of topics covered in ENVE 302 Water and Wastewater Quality. The objective of the course is to introduce the students to the various laboratory methods used in the analysis and treatment of potable water before human use and treatment of wastewater before disposing into natural water bodies. Additional fees apply.

title: Entrepreneurship Internship
description: Offers the opportunity for students to work closely with an entrepreneur of their choice as either a paid or unpaid intern. Includes orientation session before the start of the internship, midterm and final reports, and final poster presentation. It also includes the entrepreneur’s midterm and final evaluation of the student. Graded Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory.

title: Entrepreneurship Internship
description: Offers the opportunity for students to work closely with an entrepreneur of their choice as either a paid or unpaid intern. Includes orientation session before the start of the internship, midterm and final reports, and final poster presentation. It also includes the entrepreneur’s midterm and final evaluation of the student. Graded Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory.

title: Entrepreneurship Internship
description: Offers the opportunity for students to work closely with an entrepreneur of their choice as either a paid or unpaid intern. Includes orientation session before the start of the internship, midterm and final reports, and final poster presentation. It also includes the entrepreneur’s midterm and final evaluation of the student. Graded Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory.

title: Experiential Learning
description: P-I-R option [Practicum, Internship, or Research] credit assessed under this course. Each student's proposed field experience/research is to approved by the program director and the student's advisor. Field supervision is to be performed by the program director, faculty advisor or another faculty member in conjunction with the host firm, agency or department. Graded Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory.

title: Environmental Problem Solving I
description: Students (normally working in teams) pursue "original" hands-on research related to a local environmental problem. Students apply interdisciplinary knowledge to research the problem and recommend possible solutions. This portion of the course is largely devoted to investigating the problem both in the field and in the literature. Culmination of this experience is an Environmental Science 492. This course is required of all Senior Environmental Science and Environmental Studies.

title: Air Quality Laboratory
description: Laboratory course emphasizing techniques and basic principles of topics covered in ENVE 301 Air Quality. Additional fees apply.

title: Material & Energy Balance
description: Introduction to material and energy balance concepts, calculations and problem-solving skills, including environmental and biological systems. Material balance for single and multicomponent systems. Energy balance on non-reactive/reactive/transient processes.

title: Environmental Sustainability
description: Examines sources and sinks of environmental  pollution. Reviews technologies and techniques for  pollution control, waste management, and the 3  Rs: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle. Exploration of Life  Cycle Analysis of technology, techniques and  social pathways to enable a transition to  sustainability: Local to Global.

title: Politics of Global Ecology
description: Explores the range of conventions, treaties and other arrangements in international law and politics for the management and protection of global environmental resources. Particular attention is paid to the international laws regarding Antarctica, the Open Seas, the Atmosphere, Biodiversity, Transboundary Resources (including the Great Lakes, boundary river systems, and pelagic and diadromous species), and Outer Space.

title: Environmental Impact & Resource Assessment
description: History, philosophy, and legal authority for the environmental impact assessment process are reviewed in this course. Environmental site assessment and auditing are examined. Linkages between federal and state regulatory matrices and impact assessment are addressed. Methods and techniques for conduction analyses are discussed. Risk analysis, social and economic impact assessment, technology assessment, and other assessment techniques are explored. Project management and data use are considered.

title: Environmental Problem Solving II
description: Research performed in Environmental Science 491 is completed and presented in a formal seminar and in a written report. This course is required of all Senior Environmental Science and Environmental Studies majors.

title: Financial Institutions and Services
description: The course introduces the student to financial institutions from the perspectives of the financial analyst and corporate finance manager. The student will become familiar with the financial services offered to the public and with the financial, operational, and organizational aspects of the institution. Topics may vary but will usually include asset and liability management, investment and commercial banking, non-bank financial firms, mergers, acquisitions and capital market financial instruments and derivative securities. The course will include expert speakers from financial institutions, applied cases and computer-based projects.

title: Risk Management & Insurance
description: The course focuses on non-speculative risk and its management; identification and measurement of risk; techniques of risk control, models of risk management. The course also considers applications in insurance.

title: Religion & Nature
description: Religion and Nature is a survey course dealing with the diversity of religious worldviews on the natural environment. The course will begin with a broad overview of the historical and current interactions of faith and the natural world. The course reviews worldviews of indigenous religions, south Asian religions, Buddhism, Chinese Traditions, Japanese Traditions, Judaism, Christianity, Islam and Emerging Religions. The third component course area will deal with recent and emerging trends including but not limited to: ecocentrism, radical environmentalism, ecofeminism, sustainability, liberation theology, Earthkeeping, new cosmologies/Gaia, globalization, community and ecojustice. (Also listed as Religion 233.)

title: Investment Portfolio Management
description: The course takes the student through an analysis of the stock market, equity and debt investments. Topics covered include developments in investment theory, valuation principles and practices, analysis and management of equities, fixed income instruments, alternative investments and derivatives. The course includes projects using commercially-available software.

title: Environmental Policy & Law
description: Policies and politics of environmental protection and natural resource use are explored in this course. Origin and development of environmental law are examined with special emphasis on the role of the Public Trust Doctrine, Police Power, and traditions of Preservation and Conservation under law. The development and implementation of the National Environmental Policy Act and other key federal statutes are addressed. Principles governing  regulatory compliance and execution of key natural resource and environmental management tasks at the federal and state levels are considered.

title: Science, Technology & Society
description: This course examines the meaning and impact of scientific discovery and technological innovation upon society. Economic and sociological issues and impacts will be examined as well, with special emphasis on the theory of scientific revolution and diffusion of innovation. Political, managerial /economic and social leadership regarding science and technology will be examined.

title: Land Use Planning
description: This course is designed to provide students with a survey of issues in planning theory, practice and application. Programmatic and regulatory factors surrounding land-use are described and related to preservation, conservation and management of land resources. Design and implementation of the built human environment and infrastructure, and its relationship to land will be explored. Students will be shown how governments deal with environmental quality, economic development, growth management, coastal zone management and related issues in the context of land resources. Students will be exposed to principles governing regulatory compliance and execution of key land-use management tasks at the local, regional, state and national levels. To effectively accomplish this, class periods will be split between lecture and discussion.

title: International Finance
description: The course will explore challenges and opportunities in the field of international finance. An in-depth analysis will be made of the exposure to the added international financial risks of a multinational corporation including topical coverage of triangular arbitrage, currency hedging and translational effects on financial results.

title: Gender & Popular Culture
description: This will review the relationship between cultural attitudes and institutions and gender roles in our society today. General topics will include our conceptions of masculinity and femininity. We will also focus on specific influential sources such as media, educational institutions, technology, and other aspects of American society today.

title: Business Finance
description: The course examines the financial function of corporations. Emphasizes financial analysis, planning, and control; working capital management; capital budgeting; valuation, cost of capital, and leverage; and long and short-term financing decisions.

title: Inner Solar System Geology
description:
Designed for non-majors, this course is a general
survey of basic earth science and planetary
science topics. Students will be introduced to
basic concepts of geology, rocks and minerals,
historical, geology, plate tectonics, scale and
formation of the solar system, and basic motions
of the earth, plus celestial bodies, planetary
geology, and contemporary planetary science
missions. Focus will be placed on laboratory
techniques and procedures as well as
investigative skills to solve science-related
problems. Field and lab work will involve
investigations of geology sites and a
planetarium demonstration.

title: Introduction to Environmental Geology
description: Introduction to the science of geology as it relates to human activity. How geologic processes and hazards influence human activities; geologic aspects of pollution, and other related topics. Laboratory activities include identification of minerals and rocks, recognition of surface features on topographic maps, and integration and interpretation of this data and natural hazards.

title: Investment Fundamentals
description: this course introduces the student to the structure and function of stock market, portfolio diversification; and securities analysis.

title: Dinosaurs
description: An introduction to the fascinating world of dinosaurs through fossils, models, books and film. Travel back in time and meet the 19th century scientists who made these fossils come to life. Understand the changing concepts of dinosaurs and the important scientific discoveries supporting these changes. Learn how paleontologists interpret where, when and how dinosaurs lived. Recognize the main groups of dinosaurs and their unique evolutionary characteristics. Learn the anatomy and classification of 35+ common dinosaurs.

title: Finance Internship
description: Offers opportunity for field experience in finance through either a paid or unpaid internship experience with cooperating organizations. Includes orientation session prior to the start of the internship and debriefing seminar once during the semester. Includes site supervisor evaluation(s) of student, a student evaluation of their internship experience, and a completed portfolio and presentation. Graded Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory.

title: American Leaders in Gender Issues
description: This course examines the writings and lives of American gender leaders from the Seneca Falls Convention through the present. Particular attention will be paid to the work done by Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Susan B. Anthony, Charlotte Perkins Gilman, Ann Douglas Wood, Betty Friedan, Gloria Steinem, Camille Paglia, Naomi Wolf, and Susan Faludi. Major leaders in the American Gay Rights Movement will also be studied. Opposing perspectives will be examined and students will be challenged to think about their viewpoints, and how they can become leaders in the struggle to ensure equality for everyone's rights.

title: Dinosaur Lab
description: This laboratory experience is presented as an apprenticeship in the field of vertebrate paleontology. Jurassic dinosaur and other vertebrate faunal material collected in Utah will be prepared and studied. Lab exercises include the instruction for the proper use of tools, technique application and procedures to prepare fossil dinosaur material for archival storage and/or study. Application of the scientific method using vertebrate data and geologic principles will also be part of the laboratory experience. A comprehensive scientific report is required. Additional fees apply.

title: Representations in American Film
description: This course examines the evolution of the film industry and its power in shaping beliefs and attitudes towards people of various genders, sexualities, races, and (dis)abilities. Students will critically analyze how people and cultures are represented in historic and contemporary films, culminating in a research project that examines a representation / genre of the student’s choosing. Students will be required to watch several films outside of class.

title: Advanced Gender & Sexuality Studies
description: This course will serve as a capstone experience of the Gender and Sexuality studies program as it examines the breadth and depth of gender and sexuality research. Students will read essays and research in areas of social construction, representation, identity, power/oppression, intersectionality, and the body. Students will conduct their own local experiential education praxis project (20 hours) that will result in a program to promote social change.

title: Historical Geology
description: History of earth traced from beginning to present, emphasizing geology of North America with a focus on tectonic, paleogeographic and paleoclimatic changes through time. There is a focus on understanding environments where sediments are deposited. Evolution of major groups of animals and plants stressed. Laboratory work consists of building skills necessary to make observations and interpretations about the history of the earth. Additional fees apply.

title: Structural Geology
description: The description, classification and mechanics of fractures, faults, folds and related structures of the Earth's crust; the concepts of stress, strain and tectonics; the determination of structure from geologic maps, and the techniques of structural analysis. Field trip required as part of this course.

title: Demonstration Field Trip
description: One-to-two week field trip taken in May to various localities in United States or Canada. Written reports required. Additional fees apply.

title: Physical Geography
description: Topics covered include spatial science and the geographer's mapping tools, the Earth's global energy balance, the oceans and atmosphere and their role in the Earth's climate and its weather systems, global climate change and its impact on society, earth materials emphasizing soils, and landforms made by volcanic, tectonic, weathering, mass wasting, running water, waves, wind, and glacial processes.

title: Sedimentation & Stratigraphy
description: Fundamental principles of sedimentation and stratigraphy. Sedimentary processes, products, and structure to interpret depositional conditions and history necessary for environmental interpretation and resource exploration. Field trip required as part of this course.

title: Physical Geology
description: Minerals and rocks; natural processes operative in forming surface of earth and structural features of earth's crust. Laboratory work consists of study of common minerals and rocks and use of topographic maps and aerial photos in  interpretation of surface features. Additional fees apply.

title: Invertebrate Paleontology
description: Systematic classification of invertebrate fossils, their geologic range, distribution, and habitat, and use of guide fossils in age determination of geologic formations. Field trip required as part of this course.

title: Igneous & Metamorphic Petrology
description: This course will apply hand-sample and microscopic mineral identification skills learned in GEOL 202 to igneous and metamorphic rocks.  Along with basic information on classification and nomenclature for igneous and metamorphic rocks, the following topics will also be broached: petrogenesis of various compositions of magmas, ternary phase diagrams, major, minor and trace element geochemistry, volcanic igneous structures and field relationships, metamorphic facies and equilibrium mineral assemblages, and metamorphism of pelitic, ultramafic and carbonate rocks.  The three hour lab periods will focus on igneous and metamorphic petrography, and several igneous case studies, with accompanying lab reports, will be conducted.

title: Earth Resources
description: A comprehensive study of earth resources including fossil fuels, alternative fuels, metals, and industrial rocks and mineral resources and environmental impact of resource exploitation and use.

title: Environmental Engineering Geology
description: Introduction to engineering properties of soils and rocks, their classification, and geological site investigations. This course also covers other topics such as landslide and slope stability analysis and practices applied in environmental and engineering projects. Laboratory exercises will introduce with hands-on examples and activities.

title: Mineralogy & Optical Mineralogy
description: This course will provide a comprehensive study of the formation, classification and identification of minerals on Earth.  The course will focus on, mineral hand sample identification, crystal chemistry, and crystallography, after which optical mineralogy will be introduced.  The weekly three-hour laboratory includes hands-on analysis of minerals, identification using a petrographic microscope, and analyses of mineral structures utilizing imaging software.

title: Geology Field Methods
description:
Traditional geology field methods will be
introduced, as will ethical components to
careers in the geosciences.

title: Introduction to GIS Mapping & Analysis
description: An introduction to Geographic Information Systems with emphasis on practical applications to earth and environmental science. A "hands-on" course in which participants use GIS software to complete a series of projects.

title: Geology Field Camp
description:
Geology Field camp is a 5-week, intensive summer
field methods course, typically taught in the
Rocky Mountain region, taught in coordination
with Albion College. Experience in geologic
mapping is the main goal of the course with
instruction focused on traditional field
methods: mapping on topographic and aerial photo
bases at a variety of scales is central to our
instruction.  Students are also introduced to
modern electronic mapping techniques, such as
GPS-assisted digital mapping. Students also gain
experience in taking detailed field notes,
measuring stratigraphic sections, onstructing geologic cross-sections, deciphering geologic
structures, and writing geologic reports.

title: Directed Research Writing
description: A continuation of GEOL 411 in which students revise, amend, or append to, their capstone research project and prepare a formal written report.

title: Intermediate Geographic Information Systems
description: This course consists of readings, hands-on projects, and discussions centered on the ways geographic information systems facilitate data analysis and communication to address common geographic problems. Students will learn to use GIS software to analyze both vector and raster data using a variety of techniques, including spatial and attribute queries, map overlay, and buffering and will gain experience in designing and producing effective maps. Students will apply their skills in a number of assignments and will examine the ethical dimensions of the production of and access to GIS data.

title: Environmental Hydrogeology
description: Principles and practices in applied hydrogeology. This course covers fundamental concepts about groundwater occurrence, movement through porous media, aquifer properties, contamination, and management issues.

title: Petroleum Geology
description: The geological aspects of the petroleum system that are important to exploration and development used in evaluation of petroleum reservoirs. Includes techniques of subsurface mapping; theories of origin evolution, migration, and trapping of hydrocarbons; introduction to reflection seismology. Emphasis on geographic and geologic localities of current industrial interest. Practical problems and mapping exercises included.

title: Software for Design
description: This course will teach proficiency in the Adobe software suite including Photoshop, Illustrator and InDesign. Concentration on the development of technical skills by enabling students to use the computer as a tool for solving design problems. Instruction will include lectures, exercises and projects to assist students in the learning process. Additional fees apply.

title: Directed Research
description: A directed field or laboratory based research project on some aspect or problem in geology. Students must present an oral summary of their project results.  All projects must be initiated with a proposal and approved by the instructor. Students should begin planning for this course by consulting with a faculty member early in the semester prior to their senior year.

title: Interaction Design
description: This course will inform you of web design standards, trends, and techniques for designing interfaces for websites and mobile applications. It will provide you with the basic software knowledge for interface prototyping and other software for building interfaces. In addition, students will learn basic writing and implementation of Cascading Style Sheets (CSS), HTML, and JavaScript. Emphasis will be weighed heavily on design for the User Experience (UX). Additional fees apply.

title: Intro to GIS Mapping & Analysis
description: An introduction to Geographic Information Systems with emphasis on practical applications to earth and environmental science. A "hands-on" course in which participants use GIS software to complete a series of projects.

title: Information Design
description:
This studio course explores the method of graphic design to create an accurate visual representation
of specific information. Students will learn how to create a visual representation of a complex idea that
improves legibility and comprehension by the viewer. The course will introduce forms of information
design, such as icon development, time lines and maps, as well as the data visualization of diagrams and
graphs. Instruction will guide students through the methods of effectively designing information
graphics with industry standard software. Lectures and readings will prepare students to complete a
series of projects that will be applicable to any area of study. Additional Fees may apply.

title: Design Studio Workshop
description: This course concentrates on the research, design, and production of complex design projects implemented through diverse applications. The curriculum consists of a semester-long, self-authored project, as well as a series of workshop assignments that will acclimate students to the pace of a professional design studio. Additionally, professional business practices of the creative field will be addressed and students will learn how to create custom materials needed for maintaining a business. Emphasis will be made on professional, organizational, and presentation skills. Students shall complete course with experience in how to follow an effective design process within an efficient time frame.

title: Christianity: A Global History
description: This course will be a general survey of the history of Christianity, from its origins to the present day. It will focus especially on Christianity as a global, culturally diverse religion. In addition, this course will also serve as an essential introduction to Christian scripture and theology. Also listed as Religion 111.

title: Design Foundations
description: This course explores the foundations of design elements and the application within the professional field. The class introduces students to creative thinking processes and techniques as they relate to visual communication. Students will present ideas and information on a wide range of topics through visual rather than verbal language. Students will develop fluency in visual language to assist communication in critiques and discussion. Additional fees apply.

title: Logo Design & Branding
description: This course will continue the exploration of design elements and visual communication as related to advertising and corporate identity. This class will focus on layout, color, and concept development. Additional fees apply.

title: United States History Since 1877
description: An introduction to the history of the United States from the end of Reconstruction to the modern day. It is also an introduction to the historian's craft - the challenges of historical analysis, critical thinking, and clear communication. This course will emphasize the social, economic, and political developments that shaped America's adjustments to "modernity," the implications of "globalism," and the maturation of the U.S. as a world power. Intended for students with freshman or sophomore standing.

title: Advanced Typography
description: This course further explores the creative thinking processes as they relate to visual communication. Students will develop typographic design skills in relation to multiple-page documents, grid systems, hand-lettering and the history of typography. Additional fees apply.

title: Typography
description: This course will provide a comprehensive background on conceptual and practical uses of typography within graphic design. The course will discuss typographic terminology, history, and type setting and hand-lettering techniques, as well as creative uses of typography in respect to letter form, layout, and corporate identity.  The course also will introduce students to typographic functions as they apply to industry standard software. Additional fees apply.

title: Portfolio
description: The intent of this course is to provide insight and experience in producing professional-quality presentation materials for the purpose of self-promotion in the areas of art and graphic design. Students will develop portfolio and self-promotion materials specifically targeted toward their career interests.

title: The Ancient World
description: This is an introductory level course to world civilizations that follows the story of the human race from its murky origins into the first millennium of the Common Era. The focus of this course will be on the development of civilizations, belief systems (philosophies and religions), empires, and interactions among various peoples and states. The range of topics and themes in the course will extend through political, intellectual, social, literary, economic, and ecological history.

title: United States History to 1877
